title: The RAND School Is Rethinking Higher Ed: Q&A with Nancy Staudt
gist: Promotional Q&A with RAND School dean Nancy Staudt on adapting public-policy graduate education to a fast-changing, AI-shaped world. Mostly institutional marketing; the only non-obvious signal is RAND's stated dual stance on classroom AI: actively integrate it while explicitly guarding against student over-reliance.

insights.claim: The RAND School of Public Policy is encouraging students to use AI in coursework while deliberately enforcing limits so graduates do not become dependent on it for core analytical skills.
insights.speaker: Nancy Staudt
insights.so_what: Signals how elite policy/research institutions are positioning AI as augmentation-not-substitution in talent pipelines feeding government and think tanks, relevant to ed-tech and workforce-skills theses.
insights.quote: It really is a dual approach: helping students learn to use AI effectively while also making sure guidelines are clear and limitations are widely understood.
insights.locator: How is AI affecting the way RAND School students are trained today?

insights.claim: Demand among graduate students is shifting toward applied, hands-on education over theory because students want skills that stay relevant as the world changes.
insights.speaker: Nancy Staudt
insights.so_what: Reinforces the ongoing pivot in higher-ed value propositions toward employability and applied skills, a structural headwind for purely theory-based programs.
insights.quote: That is why students are so eager for applied education.
insights.locator: What do you think students are looking for in higher education today?
